does: This paper develops a rigorous evaluation framework for assessing whether sparse autoencoders learn meaningful hierarchical structures in their discovered feature spaces. The authors derive key requirements for generalization-specialization hierarchies by synthesizing principles from semantic networks, taxonomy research, and recent SAE literature. They then apply a concrete evaluation protocol to current SAE approaches trained on visual data, measuring how well learned features organize into coherent hierarchies. Their main finding is that while SAEs produce feature spaces that contain latent hierarchical structure, explicitly establishing good hierarchical organization remains difficult in practice, with feature absorption and other mechanisms undermining hierarchy quality.
connects: This work builds on recent progress in sparse autoencoders as tools for unsupervised concept discovery in neural networks, extending earlier mechanistic interpretability efforts. It responds to growing interest in imposing hierarchical structure on learned feature spaces—either through explicit constraints or implicit training effects—as a path toward more interpretable and manageable representations. The paper connects to classical work on semantic networks and formal taxonomy design, bringing those well-established principles to bear on modern representation learning. It also engages with prior SAE research that has hinted at hierarchical organization without systematic evaluation, filling a methodological gap in how the field compares and validates hierarchical feature discovery approaches.
enables: This work establishes a foundation for future research in learning hierarchical concept representations from neural models. By clarifying requirements and providing a concrete evaluation protocol, it enables more rigorous comparison of hierarchical SAE variants and motivates development of new training methods specifically designed to improve hierarchy quality. The framework could be extended to other unsupervised discovery methods and different model types beyond visual autoencoders. It opens questions about what inductive biases or architectural modifications best support meaningful hierarchical organization, and provides tools for evaluating whether proposed solutions actually succeed. This sets up research into better mechanisms for feature specialization and inter-feature relationships that respect semantic hierarchy.
